contents: Clinical features of shiitake dermatitis: a systematic reviewAustin H. Nguyen1, MS, Maria I. Gonzaga2, BS, Victoria M. Lim1, BS, Michael J. Adler3,MD, Mario V. Mitkov4, MD, and Mark A. Cappel4, MD1Creighton University School of Medicine,Omaha, NE, USA, 2Department of Biology,Frank R. Seaver College of Science andEngineering, Loyola Marymount University,Los Angeles, CA, USA, 3PortlandDermatology Clinic, Portland, OR, USA,and 4Department of Dermatology, MayoClinic Florida, Jacksonville, FL, USACorrespondenceAustin H. Nguyen, MSCreighton University School of Medicine2500 California PlazaOmahaNE 68102USAE-mail: Funding: None.Conflicts of interest: NoneSummaryShiitake dermatitis is a rare cutaneous reaction to lentinan, a polysaccharide component inthe cell walls of shiitake mushrooms (Lentinula edodes). Herein, we systematically reviewthe case report and case series English-language literature on shiitake dermatitis, whichrefers to a total of 50 patients (38 males, 12 females; mean age: 44.58 years). Themajority of cases occurred after the consumption of raw mushrooms, whereas 22% ofcases were caused by the eating of lightly or undercooked mushrooms. The most commonclinical presentations, localized symptoms, and systemic findings include linear flagellateddermatitis (98%), pruritus (78%), and fever, diarrhea, and mucosal ulcers, respectively. Thediagnosis of this entity continues to be based on clinical findings as laboratoryabnormalities, and the findings of skin biopsies and patch/prick tests are nonspecific andinconsistent. The condition is self-limiting, resolving in approximately 12.5 d without

treatment. Based on the included case reports, it appears that medical treatment mayslightly shorten the course of disease (to 9-11 d, varying by therapy) but should beconsidered on an individual patient basis. However, the treatment of symptoms,reassurance, and the avoidance of re-exposure are sufficient treatment recommendationsfor this condition.IntroductionThe shiitake mushroom (Lentinula edodes) is the second mostcommonly consumed mushroom worldwide and has grown tobecome a globally and extensively used multipurpose ingredi-ent. In Asian cuisine, the shiitake mushroom is consumed fresh,dried, or cooked in a variety of dishes. Shiitake is also con-sumed in a pickled form. In Eastern alternative medicine, driedshiitake or extracts are thought to help with a host of conditions,including arthralgia, myalgia, fatigue, cardiopulmonary health,and even cancer.1 However, such effects have varied levels ofsupporting scientific evidence. According to the US Departmentof Agriculture, US production of shiitake mushrooms in 2014-

2015 amounted to 10.5 million pounds of shiitake mushrooms,representing US$32.6 million of revenue.2 Evidently, this mush-room is extensively consumed, making associated reactions apublic health concern. Because the mushroom is a commoningredient in Chinese and Japanese cuisine, the first docu-mented reactions to these mushrooms were noted in EasternAsia. However, the adoption of Asian cuisines worldwide hasexpanded the susceptible population and the subsequent needfor awareness of shiitake-induced reactions.Shiitake mushroom-induced toxicoderma, otherwise known asshiitake dermatitis, presents as a striking flagellate erythematouseruption in susceptible patients after the consumption of largeamounts of raw or undercooked shiitake mushrooms. The eruptionis believed to be attributable to either a toxic or hypersensitivityresponse to lentinan, a polysaccharide component found within themushroom cell wall.3 Lentinan is heat-labile, and thus eruptions arefrequently noted in patients who have consumed inadequatelycooked mushrooms. Interestingly, in trials investigating the possible

anti-tumor effects of lentinan, nine of 519 patients went on todevelop shiitake dermatitis-like cutaneous symptoms,4 further impli-cating lentinan as the causative agent of the eruption.The first case of shiitake dermatitis was noted in 1977 byNakamura.5 Since then, varying presentations and manifesta-tions of the disease have been reported. As recent mushroomconsumption is an overlooked detail in patient history, shiitakedermatitis is frequently misdiagnosed by any provider unfamiliarwith this condition. The disease is self-limiting, and hence diag-nostic procedures and treatments may be unnecessary andmay even exacerbate symptoms. Herein, we seek to consoli-date current understandings of the disease.Materials and methodsThe National Library of Medicine's PubMed database wassystematically searched (to July 2015) for articles pertainingto the topic of shiitake dermatitis. The search terms usedwere "shiitake" with "dermatitis" and "toxicoderma". Searchresults were cross-checked with the Web of Sciencedatabase using the same search terms. Article titles andabstracts were screened, and the full texts of all possiblyrelevant articles were retrieved to allow their evaluation forinclusion and the extraction of data. Exclusion criteriadenied the inclusion of articles not written in English,conference abstracts, studies not performed in primary humansubjects, and studies with inadequate patient data. Articlesdiscussing cases of allergic contact dermatitis in response toshiitake mushroom exposure were not included. Additionally,cases of systemic symptoms (i.e. asthma or gastrointestinaldistress) without dermatologic manifestations were notincluded.ResultsThe initial search of PubMed and Web of Science returned 97articles (Fig. 1). Twenty-nine articles were included, comprisinga total of 50 cases of patients with shiitake dermatitis.4,6-33 Arti-cles consisted of case reports (24 patients) and case series (26

patients). A summary of the study characteristics and patientpopulation pooled for this review is described in Table 1.EtiologyAll included cases resulted after the oral consumption of shi-itake mushroom in various forms, including raw (n = 26) andlightly or undercooked (including in soups and stir fries, n = 10).In three cases, the subjects had not experienced any reactionsto the consumption of shiitake mushrooms prior to the reportedincident: one case had previously consumed roasted mush-rooms, and another reported no reaction after consuming shi-itake mushrooms as recently as 2 weeks previously.6,28 Twocases reported recurring symptoms before diagnosis (at 1 yearand 16 years, respectively).7,9 One subject reported the dailyconsumption of raw mushrooms for 2 weeks prior to onset.33No reports investigated genetic predisposition or familial pat-terns of shiitake dermatitis (Table 2).DemographicsThe mean patient age was 44.58 years (range: 25-86 years).Incidence was more common in males at a 3 : 1 ratio (38males, 12 females). The highest number of cases was reportedin France, as documented in a systematic retrospective studyon shiitake dermatitis (n = 15) carried out through the FrenchPoison Control Centers.22 Other countries with high numbers ofcases included the USA (n = 9), Germany (n = 8), and the UK(n = 6). Asian cases are likely to be under-represented becausenon-English-language articles were excluded from this review(Table 1).Clinical presentationThe classically associated linear flagellate dermatitis was pre-sent in 98% of cases. The majority of lesions were described aspapular or urticarial (80%), with 78% of cases having associatedpruritus. Additional signs included associated petechiae (14%)or purpura (2%), plaques (6%), burning sensation (2%), blanch-ing (2%), and pustules (2%). Interestingly, one case (2%) wasCountryStudies, n (cases, n)

Austria1 (1)Brazil2 (2)China1 (1)Czech Republic1 (1)France3 (17)Germany6 (8)Italy1 (2)Japan1 (1)Poland1 (1)Singapore1 (1)United Kingdom6 (6)United States5 (9)Study designCase reports24 patientsCase series5 (2, 3, 3, 3, 15 patients)Demographics (n = 50)Total patients, n50Male, n (%)38 (76.0%)Age, years, mean (range)44.58 (25-86)described as morbilliform in presentation. Systemic signs andsymptoms were less common and included fever (6%), diarrhea(4%), paresthesia of the hands (2%), urticarial dermographism(2%), mouth ulcer/mucosal involvement (4%), dysphagia (2%),and conjunctivitis (2%).Nearly all cases involved the trunk, and the majority ofpatients had upper and/or lower limb involvement (70% and66%, respectively). Involvement of the face and neck was lesscommon (22% and 12%, respectively), and only two or threecases involved the hands and feet. The average time to onsetof symptoms was 42.4 h (range: 2-120 h) after the consump-tion of shiitake mushrooms (Table 2).WorkupBiopsy was performed in 19 cases (Table 3). Histologic exami-nation generally consisted of spongiosis, with dermal edemaand occasional hyper- or parakeratosis. Lymphocytic perivascu-lar infiltration (with occasional lymphocytic exocytosis) was gen-erallyseenwitheosinophilsandoccasionallyneutrophils.

Keratinocyte necrosis was seen in a minority of cases. Interest-ingly, one case was uniquely described as lichenoid eczema-tous dermatitis.Laboratory workup was performed in nearly half of cases(n = 22). Of note, three patients presented with blood cell countabnormalities,includingeosinophilia(n = 2),neutrophilia(n = 2), and leukocytosis (n = 1). Additionally, values that wereelevated included those for C-reactive protein (CRP) (n = 2),serum immunoglobulin E (IgE) (n = 3), and lactate dehydroge-nase (n = 1). Specific tests for allergy were not frequently per-formed. Skin tests were inconsistent indicators of shiitakedermatitis: patch testing demonstrated one positive result of thetwo performed, and prick testing demonstrated two positiveresults of the four performed. Oral rechallenge induced symp-toms in all patients tested (n = 3).In general, shiitake dermatitis was self-limiting, and treatmentwas focused on symptomatic management. A total of 31patients were prescribed corticosteroids (topical, n = 14; sys-

temic, n = 8; not all studies specified which), and 21 patientsreceived antihistamines. Of those receiving treatment, 13 caseswere reported to have no relief of symptoms, and one patientreported worsening of symptoms.17 Twelve patients received notreatment. Other treatment approaches included polidocanolandpsoralenultravioletA(PUVA)therapy.Onepatientattempted treatment with valacyclovir without relief, prior to adiagnosisbeingestablished.Treatmentmodalitywasnotreported in two cases. All cases resulted in complete resolution(two cases did not report outcomes or were lost from follow-up)within an average of 10.74 d (range: 2-35 d; adequate data in37 cases). The average time to resolution in cases not receivingtreatment was 12.5 d (range: 7-28 d; n = 8). In subjects receiv-ing steroid monotherapy, the average time to resolution was11.2 d (range: 2-35 d; n = 19) in patients receiving topical for-mulations and 9.3 d (range: 7-14 d; n = 4) in those receivingsystemic steroids. The mean time to resolution in subjectsreceiving antihistamine and steroid combination therapy was

10.4 d (range: 2-21 d; n = 17). No cases reported antihistaminemonotherapy.Exposure to mushrooms (n = 46), n (%)Raw26 (56.5%)Lightly or undercooked10 (21.7%)Powder (medicinal)1 (2.2%)Large amount2 (4.3%)Log-grown1 (2.2%)Dried4 (8.7%)Time to onset, h, (n = 43) mean (range)42.4 (2-120)Cutaneous manifestations (n = 50), n (%)Flagellate/linear49 (98.0%)Urticarial papules40 (80.0%)Pruritus39 (78.0%)Petechiae7 (14.0%)Plaques3 (6.0%)Distribution (n = 50), n (%)Back45 (90.0%)Abdomen/chest43 (86.0%)Upper extremity35 (70.0%)Lower extremity33 66.0%)Face11 (22.0%)Neck6 (12.0%)Hands3 (6.0%)Feet2 (4.0%)n-values represent cases for which adequate data were reported.n (%)Biopsy findings (n = 19)Lymphoid infiltrate17 (89.5%)Eosinophils12 (63.2%)Epidermal spongiosis11 (57.9%)Dermal edema11 (57.9%)Hyper-/parakeratosis6 (31.6%)Lymphocyte exocytosis5 (26.3%)Neutrophils3 (15.8%)Keratinocyte necrosis3 (15.8%)Management (n = 47)No treatment given12 (25.5%)Worsening or no relief with treatment14 (40.0%)aCorticosteroid31 (88.6%)aAntihistamine21 (60.0%)aaOf those given treatment (n = 35).International Journal of Dermatology 2017, 56, 610-616a 2017 The International Society of Dermatology 13654632, 2017, 6, Downloaded from  by University Of Toronto Libraries, Wiley Online Library on [06/07/2023]. See the Terms and Conditions ( on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons LicenseDiscussionThe overall clinical picture of shiitake dermatitis is fairly consis-tent. The condition presents approximately 40 h after the con-sumptionofshiitakemushroomswithpruritic,linear,erythematous, urticarial papules and plaques (flagellate ery-thema), and the subsequent development of linear gray-brown

patches (flagellate hyperpigmentation) (Fig. 2). The differentialdiagnosis primarily includes bleomycin-induced flagellate hyper-pigmentation and dermatomyositis flagellate erythema. How-ever, manifestations can vary in presentation and may alsoinclude phytophotodermatitis, digitate dermatosis and, rarely,morbilliform drug eruption.16Shiitake dermatitis occurs in a unique clinical setting in whichpresumably genetically susceptible individuals are exposed tolarge amounts of uncooked or undercooked mushrooms: a totalof 36 of the eruptions included in our review material occurredin this setting.6,7,12,14-16,18-23,26,30,33 This introduces the conceptthat inadequate cooking is a possible determinant of the occur-rence of shiitake dermatitis. Shiitake mushrooms contain ab-glucan in their cell walls called lentinan, which is heat-labile.3Further experiments have substantiated this theory, demonstrat-ing a cutaneous response to the consumption of shiitakemushrooms cooked at 100 degC but not to those cooked at

150 degC, a finding that associates the inherent properties of lenti-nan with the clinicopathologic features of shiitake dermatitis.26Some authors claim that lentinan produces a predictable anddose-dependentresponse,representingatoxicmecha-nism,4,9,34 whereas others postulate an unpredictable, immune-mediated response, occurring in genetically predisposed individ-uals, independently of dose, representing a hypersensitivitymechanism.26,28 In support of the hypersensitivity theory, Cor-azza et al.26 demonstrated positive patch testing in a patientpreviously diagnosed with shiitake dermatitis using mushroomscooked at 100 degC for 15 min, an irreproducible result when per-formed in five healthy, unsensitized volunteers.a 2017 The International Society of DermatologyInternational Journal of Dermatology 2017, 56, 610-616 13654632, 2017, 6, Downloaded from  by University Of Toronto Libraries, Wiley Online Library on [06/07/2023]. See the Terms and Conditions ( on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons LicenseConversely, the fact that this rash usually occurs after theingestion of large amounts of raw mushroom and not after lim-ited exposures suggests that there may be a dose-dependenteffect, which is consistent with a toxic mechanism.7,18,19,28 How-ever, we favor a hypersensitivity reaction and postulate that

genetically susceptible individuals with certain human leukocyteantigen (HLA) alleles require exposure to large amounts of lenti-nan in order to be sensitized. This phenomenon has been notedin the sensitization process of individuals using squaric acid, inwhich the cutaneous hypersensitivity reaction has been found tobe dose-dependent.35 Furthermore, recent studies have identi-fied certain HLA alleles and cytokine profiles that render hostsprone to producing cutaneous hypersensitivity responses togiven antigens.36,37 Although no specific HLA allele(s) havebeen identified in those who mount a hypersensitivity responseto shiitake mushrooms, it is plausible to infer that an associationmay exist.Lentinan has also been shown to induce interleukin-1 (IL-1),IL-2, and tumor necrosis factor-a (TNF-a) secretion, nitric oxiderelease, vasodilation, and hemorrhage, skewing the immunesystem towards a Th1-type response.38-43 Rats treated withoral lentinan for 4 weeks exhibited a significantly higher T celllevel, helper T cell level and helper to suppressor T cell ratio,

and a significantly lower suppressor T cell level than did thecontrol group.44 Lentinan has also been studied as adjuvanttherapy for immune stimulation in cancer patients. Treatment ofdigestive cancer patients with lentinan increased blood CD4+interferon-c+(IFN-c+)T cellpercentages,whiledecreasinglevels of IL-4 and IL-6 expressing T cells.45 These immune-modulating properties of lentinan are suggestive of a Th1 hyper-sensitivity mechanism underlying shiitake dermatitis (Fig. 3).The distribution of linear, flagellate papules and vesicles islikely to be Koebner-induced,34 a phenomenon characterized bythe reproduction of lesions after trauma to the skin. AlthoughInternational Journal of Dermatology 2017, 56, 610-616a 2017 The International Society of Dermatology 13654632, 2017, 6, Downloaded from  by University Of Toronto Libraries, Wiley Online Library on [06/07/2023]. See the Terms and Conditions ( on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons Licensesome reports were unable to reproduce lesions by scratching,9Ade et al.28 were able to clearly demonstrate the cutaneousreproduction of patients' initials 44 h after rechallenge withmushrooms, without the involvement of protected skin. Ade

et al.28 proposed that timing is a critical element and that themost intense eruptions occur while scratching during periods ofhigh lentinan serum levels. Furthermore, minor trauma, such asscratching,isthoughttoincreasevascularpermeability.Together with high serum levels, this could allow greater cuta-neous lentinan deposition and thus leukocyte activation andinflammation in these areas.46 The residual post-inflammatoryhyperpigmentation is thought to result from superficial dermaldeposition of melanin (pigment incontinence from the epidermis)and hemosiderin (red blood cell extravasation from inflamedblood vessels).28 This is also consistent with proposed mecha-nisms of bleomycin-induced flagellate erythema.46likely to be sufficient in most cases. In fact, 28% of the patientsreferred to in the present review material reported no alleviationof symptoms with treatment and even the worsening of symp-toms in one case. Treatment generally consisted of antihista-mines and topical steroids, although systemic steroids may be

considered. Based on the case reports included, it appears thatthe average time to clearance is shorter in patients treated withsystemic steroids (9.3 d; range: 7-14 d) compared with patientswho receive no treatment (12.5 d; range: 7-28 d). However, thishas not been substantiated by controlled trials, and thesereports are not sufficiently powered to warrant the recommenda-tion of systemic steroids in all patients with shiitake dermatitis.Therapy options should be tailored on a case-by-case basis,and systemic steroids should be regarded as an option forpatients with the most severe symptoms. Interestingly, Scheibaet al.12 reported the use of balneo-PUVA therapy in a patientpresenting with shiitake dermatitis. Complete resolution wasseen at 10 d after ingestion (4-5 d after the initiation of ther-apy). In the context of the average of 10.74 d (range: 2-35 d)to resolution identified in the present review, the benefits of theuse of PUVA therapy in the treatment of shiitake dermatitis areunclear.ConclusionsShiitake dermatitis is a rare condition presenting with pruritic,linear, erythematous, urticarial papules and plaques approxi-mately 40 h after the ingestion of large amounts of raw shiitakemushrooms, which resolves with flagellate hyperpigmentation. Itis thought to be caused by a hypersensitivity reaction tolentinan, contained in shiitake mushrooms. Generally, the find-ings of laboratory studies are not significant and are nonspeci-fic. Biopsy is generally not necessary as a thorough evaluationof patient history and physical examination are sufficient for clin-ical diagnosis. The condition is self-limiting, although treatmentwith steroids and antihistamines may expedite the course of dis-ease and/or alleviate symptoms.References
